worth_it_explanation: Naming hygiene decides whether a publisher can slice reporting by placement, by demand source, and by deal without guessing what each label means. PubMatic and the OpenWrap header-bidding setup expose ad tags, placements, and deals through names the publisher chooses, and those names flow straight into the reporting dimensions. When the names are inconsistent, the reporting reads as a wall of strings that no one can group, so revenue questions take longer to answer and some never get answered. This check is a review of names a publisher already has rather than a rebuild, so it costs reading time, and the finding points to a concrete renaming the publisher can schedule.
intro: A naming convention is the rule a publisher follows when labeling ad tags, placements, line items, and deals so that every name carries the same fields in the same order. In a PubMatic and OpenWrap setup those names become reporting dimensions, so a parseable convention lets the publisher group revenue by site, by ad unit, by format, by demand source, or by deal in a few clicks, while inconsistent names force manual matching. This subcategory is about whether the naming convention exists and is followed across tags, placements, and deals, not about the auction mechanics themselves. These checks confirm that the names a publisher relies on for reporting are consistent and identifiable rather than ad hoc.

audit_checks.title: Ad tag and placement names do not follow a consistent, parseable convention
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When ad tag and placement names carry no consistent structure, the names still appear as reporting dimensions, so the data is present but cannot be grouped cleanly by site, ad unit, format, or position. The publisher can still read totals, so revenue is not lost directly, but every slice of analysis costs manual matching and some questions go unanswered because the labels do not line up.

educate.base:
An ad tag or placement name in PubMatic is more than a label a person reads. It is a value that flows into reporting as a dimension, so when a publisher wants to compare revenue by site, by ad unit, by format, or by screen position, those comparisons lean on the names being structured the same way every time. A name like 'home_top_300x250_desktop' can be parsed into site section, position, size, and device, so a report can group on any one of those fields. A name like 'new tag 2' or 'homepage banner final' carries the same impression data but no structure, so the data cannot be sliced without someone matching names by hand.

The pattern that causes trouble is drift rather than a single bad name. One person labels placements by size, another by page, a third by the campaign that prompted the tag, and over time the names stop sharing any common shape. Reporting then shows a list of strings that read clearly to the person who made each one and as noise to everyone else, including the auditor and any future analyst who inherits the account.

A naming convention fixes this by fixing the fields and their order in advance, so every name carries the same parts in the same sequence. A workable convention usually encodes the parts a publisher reports on most, for example site or section, ad unit or position, size, and device or environment, separated by a consistent delimiter. The header-bidding community follows the same idea on the ad server side, where the recommended line item format encodes the wrapper, media type, bidder, and price in a fixed order so the names parse, as Prebid's line item creation guidance describes for OpenWrap-style setups.

A few signs point to ad tag and placement names that lack a convention:
  • Names mix styles, so some encode size, some encode the page, and some are sequential like 'tag 3' with no parseable fields
  • No written convention exists that states which fields a name must carry and in what order
  • Reporting on a placement dimension returns labels that cannot be grouped without manual matching
You may want to confirm that a written convention exists and that current names follow it. One approach is to define the fields and delimiter the publisher reports on most, then rename placements to match the convention going forward and on a schedule for the existing ones.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, placements span many sections, templates, and positions, so the names carry more weight as the main way to tell a section-front unit from an article-body unit in reporting. You may want a convention that encodes section, template, position, and size so revenue can be grouped by where on the site the impression served, rather than by a label that only its creator can decode.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, placements sit on product pages, category pages, and editorial pages that command different prices, so the names should make page type readable in reporting. You may want a convention that encodes page type alongside position and size so the higher-value commercial inventory can be separated from editorial inventory when reading revenue.

learn_more.source_title: Line Item Manager for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's detailed configuration documentation for ad tag and placement setup is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the ad tag and placement names across the inventory and look for whether they share a consistent set of fields in a fixed order, and whether a written convention exists that defines them.

pubmatic_ui.label: PubMatic Inventory UI
steps: Open the inventory section and list the active ad tags and placements with their names.
Read across the names and note which fields each one encodes, for example site, position, size, or device, and whether those fields appear in a consistent order.
Record any names that carry no parseable fields, such as sequential or one-off labels, and the share of placements that fall outside any pattern.
Ask the publisher whether a written naming convention exists, and if so record what fields it requires and whether current names follow it.

pubmatic_reporting.label: PubMatic Analytics reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by the ad tag or placement dimension and read the labels as they appear.
Try to group the report by a field such as position or size, and note whether the names allow that grouping or whether they have to be matched by hand.
Record the cases where reporting cannot be sliced cleanly because the names do not share a structure.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a written naming convention exists for ad tags and placements and what share of current names follow it. A setup where a convention is defined and the names carry the same fields in the same order, so reporting can be grouped on those fields, passes. A setup where names mix styles or carry no parseable fields, so a placement dimension cannot be grouped without manual matching, is a fail recorded with the placements that fall outside the convention.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a naming convention exists and what share of ad tag and placement names follow it above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} labels ad tags and placements without a consistent, parseable naming convention, so the names carry no shared structure for reporting. The impression data is present, but a placement dimension cannot be grouped by site, position, size, or device without matching names by hand, which leaves some revenue questions unanswered. {{voice_recommend}} defining a written convention that fixes the fields and their order, for example site, position, size, and device separated by a consistent delimiter, then renaming placements to follow it going forward and scheduling the existing ones for a pass. After the rename, confirm that reporting can be sliced on each of those fields without manual matching.

audit_checks.title: Deal and package names are inconsistent or not identifiable
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal that cannot be identified by its name is hard to attribute revenue to, hard to troubleshoot when delivery drops, and hard to reconcile against what a buyer agreed to. Because deals carry negotiated terms and direct buyer relationships, an unidentifiable deal name raises the chance that a deal underdelivers or clears at the wrong price without anyone noticing which deal is at fault, so the cost runs higher than a placement label mix-up.

educate.base:
A deal in PubMatic represents a negotiated agreement between the publisher and a buyer, carried in the auction by a Deal ID that the OpenRTB specification defines as the unique identifier for that direct deal, as described in IAB Tech Lab's OpenRTB 2.6 specification. The Deal ID is a machine identifier, so the human-readable name a publisher gives the deal or package is what makes it possible to tell at a glance which buyer, which inventory, and which terms a given deal covers. When that name is clear, reporting and troubleshooting both become a matter of reading the label. When it is not, every question about a deal starts with detective work.

The trouble shows up in three places. Attribution suffers because revenue reported against a deal cannot be tied back to the buyer or the agreement without cross-referencing a separate record. Troubleshooting suffers because when a deal stops delivering, the team has to identify which deal is affected before they can act, and an opaque name slows that down. Reconciliation suffers because matching delivery against what a buyer agreed to depends on knowing which deal corresponds to which contract, and a name like 'PMP deal 2' or 'test deal copy' does not carry that link.

A naming convention for deals fixes the same way it does for placements, by encoding the fields a publisher needs to read most often in a fixed order. For deals those fields usually include the buyer or agency, the deal type such as a private marketplace or a preferred deal, the inventory or audience the deal targets, and a date or term marker where it helps. A consistent convention also makes it easier to spot a stale deal that should have been paused and a duplicate that was copied during setup and never cleaned up.

A few signs point to deal names that are not identifiable:
  • Deal names do not encode the buyer, the deal type, or the inventory, so the buyer behind a deal cannot be read from its name
  • Sequential or placeholder names such as 'deal 4' or 'test deal' appear among live deals
  • Reporting on the deal dimension returns labels that cannot be reconciled against the buyer agreements without a separate lookup
You may want to confirm that deal and package names identify the buyer, the type, and the inventory at a glance. One approach is to define a deal naming convention with those fields, rename the active deals to follow it, and retire any placeholder or duplicate deals the review surfaces.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, private marketplace and preferred deals often map to specific sections or sponsorship-adjacent inventory that a buyer asked for by name. You may want deal names to encode the buyer and the section or package so a sponsorship deal can be told apart from a run-of-site deal in reporting without opening each one.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, deals may target product or category inventory that carries higher value than editorial inventory, and a buyer may run separate deals for each. You may want deal names to encode the buyer and the inventory type so the higher-value commercial deals can be read and reconciled separately from broader deals.

learn_more.source_title: OpenRTB 2.6 Specification (Deal object and Deal ID)

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's detailed configuration documentation for deals and packages is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the active deal and package names and look for whether each one identifies the buyer, the deal type, and the inventory, and whether placeholder or duplicate deals are present among the live ones.

pubmatic_ui.label: PubMatic Deals UI
steps: Open the deals or inventory section and list the active deals and packages with their names and Deal IDs.
For each deal, note whether the name identifies the buyer or agency, the deal type such as a private marketplace or preferred deal, and the inventory or audience it targets.
Record any sequential, placeholder, or duplicate names among the live deals, such as 'deal 4', 'test deal', or a copied deal that was never renamed.
Ask the publisher whether a deal naming convention exists, and record what fields it requires and whether current deals follow it.

pubmatic_reporting.label: PubMatic Analytics reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by the deal dimension with revenue and impressions.
Read the deal labels and note whether each can be tied to a buyer agreement from the name alone or whether a separate lookup is needed.
Record the deals whose names cannot be reconciled against the buyer relationship, and any deals with revenue that no longer map to an active agreement.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each active deal and package, whether the name identifies the buyer, the deal type, and the inventory, and whether any placeholder or duplicate deals appear among the live ones. A setup where deal names follow a convention that makes the buyer and terms readable, so revenue can be attributed and reconciled from the name, passes. A setup where deal names are sequential, placeholder, or otherwise unidentifiable, so a deal cannot be tied to its buyer without a separate lookup, is a fail recorded with the deals affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether each active deal name identifies the buyer, type, and inventory, and whether placeholder deals are present, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} carries deal and package names that do not identify the buyer, the deal type, or the inventory, so a deal cannot be read from its name. Because deals hold negotiated terms and direct buyer relationships, an unidentifiable name makes revenue hard to attribute, delivery problems hard to trace to the right deal, and reconciliation against the buyer agreement slower than it should be. {{voice_recommend}} defining a deal naming convention that encodes the buyer, the deal type, and the inventory, renaming the active deals to follow it, and retiring any placeholder or duplicate deals the review surfaces. After the change, confirm that each deal can be tied to its buyer and terms from the name alone in reporting.
